title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma vs. Infosys BPM Limited
keyArguments: The termination was based on a valid domestic inquiry conducted in accordance with the principles of natural justice and the company's standing orders.
The employee was found guilty of misappropriation of confidential client data in violation of the employment contract and the Information Technology Act, 2000.
The Labour Court had erred in reinstating the employee without properly appreciating the gravity of the misconduct and the evidence on record.
The employer had followed due procedure under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946, issuing a proper charge sheet and conducting a fair inquiry.
The employee's conduct constituted a fundamental breach of trust rendering continued employment untenable.
judgmentSummary: The Karnataka High Court set aside the Labour Court's order of reinstatement and upheld the termination of the respondent-employee by Infosys BPM Limited. The Court found that the domestic inquiry was conducted fairly, the charges of misappropriation of confidential client data were substantiated by evidence, and the punishment of dismissal was proportionate to the gravity of the misconduct. The writ petition filed by the employer was allowed.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU

Writ Petition No. 14872 of 2022

BETWEEN:
Infosys BPM Limited, a company incorporated under the Companies Act, 1956, having its registered office at Electronics City, Bengaluru - 560 100, represented by its Authorized Signatory ... Petitioner

AND:
Rajesh Kumar Sharma, S/o Mohan Lal Sharma, Aged about 34 years, residing at No. 45, 3rd Cross, Koramangala, Bengaluru - 560 034 ... Respondent

DATE OF JUDGMENT: 14th March, 2023

HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S. VENKATARAMAN

JUDGMENT

1. This writ petition is filed under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 22nd August, 2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, IV Additional Labour Court, Bengaluru in I.D. No. 237 of 2020, whereby the Labour Court directed reinstatement of the respondent-employee with 50% back wages.

2. The brief facts giving rise to this petition are that the respondent was employed as a Senior Process Executive with the petitioner-company since 15th January, 2015. On 7th March, 2019, it came to the petitioner's notice that the respondent had accessed and transmitted confidential client data belonging to a major banking client to his personal email account, which was in clear violation of the employment contract, the company's Code of Conduct, and the provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000.

3. A charge sheet was issued to the respondent on 12th March, 2019, followed by a domestic inquiry conducted by an independent Inquiry Officer. The respondent was given full opportunity to present his defense and cross-examine witnesses. The Inquiry Officer, upon appreciation of evidence including electronic records and server access logs, found the respondent guilty of the charges levelled against him. Based on the inquiry report, the petitioner passed an order of dismissal on 30th June, 2019.

4. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the IV Additional Labour Court, Bengaluru. The Labour Court, by its impugned award, held that while the charges were proved, the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate and ordered reinstatement with 50% back wages.

5. This Court has carefully examined the inquiry proceedings and the findings of the Inquiry Officer. It is well settled that the scope of judicial review in disciplinary matters is limited. The Court does not sit as an appellate authority over the findings of a domestic inquiry. Reference is made to the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in B.C. Chaturvedi vs. Union of India, (1995) 6 SCC 749, wherein it was categorically held that if the inquiry has been fairly conducted and the findings are based on evidence, the punishment imposed by the employer cannot ordinarily be interfered with.

6. In the present case, the misconduct involves unauthorized access and transmission of sensitive banking client data. Such acts not only expose the employer to enormous civil and criminal liability but also constitute a fundamental breach of trust. The petitioner operates in a sector where data confidentiality is of paramount importance. The Labour Court committed a manifest error in substituting its own view regarding the quantum of punishment without recording any finding that the dismissal was shockingly disproportionate.

7. This Court is of the considered opinion that the Labour Court exceeded its jurisdiction in interfering with the punishment of dismissal. The gravity of the misconduct, the sensitivity of the data involved, and the clear breach of contractual obligations justify the punishment imposed. The award of the Labour Court is therefore unsustainable in law.

8. Accordingly, this writ petition is allowed. The impugned award dated 22nd August, 2022 passed by the IV Additional Labour Court, Bengaluru is hereby quashed. The order of dismissal dated 30th June, 2019 passed by the petitioner is restored. No order as to costs.

SD/-
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S. VENKATARAMAN
